Journal of Industrial Information Integration

Volume 33, June 2023, 100465

A model-based methodology to support systems security design and assessment

Author links open overlay panel
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jii.2023.100465Get rights and content
Under a Creative Commons license
open access

Highlights

  • •
    Introducing an integrative, model-based methodology to support systems security design and assessment.
  • •
    Presenting case studies that illustrate possible applications of the new methodology and can be easily generalized.

Abstract

Addressing cybersecurity aspects while designing systems is challenging. As our systems increasingly rely on digital technology to perform, security and resilience aspects need to be considered during the system design process. However, the integration of pertinent information into the systems engineering lifecycle is not trivial, as it is characterized by following verbose guidelines and documentation, and has no practical, model-based methodology to support threat-aware design of systems. In this article, we address this gap by presenting an integrative, model-based methodology to support the design and assessment of systems' security aspects. We discuss the methodology's design, specifically with respect to system development scenarios, and detail industrial case studies demonstrating the applicability of the methodology.
  • Previous article in issue
  • Next article in issue

Keywords

Model-based design
Systems security engineering
Systems specification methodology
Modeling methodology
Threat and risk assessment

1. Introduction

Addressing systems security by design is a significant systems engineering (SE) challenge which requires effective and rigorous tools. Modern systems increasingly rely on digital technology to operate. Such technology includes software components, programmable elements and communication interfaces that may be used to compromise a system's mission or operation, by affecting aspects of its confidentiality, integrity and availability. Systems security engineering (SSE) is the discipline concerned with the design of trustworthy and secure systems, in order to mitigate such risks [1].
SSE has matured as a discipline, to provide extensive technical management guidelines for systems engineers [2]. However, as both the threat landscape and the system design typically evolve throughout the development lifecycle, there is a crucial need for sustainable mechanisms to integrate, communicate and manage information regarding the system design with respect to security risks. This should be performed in a way which can be understood and shared between the systems security engineer as the security analyst, the principal systems engineer as the system designer, as well as with other stakeholders such as directors, regulators and risk managers. Furthermore, an approach addressing these multiple perspectives needs to accommodate different expert information and its integration.
In this article, we present the first field-proven, model-based methodology to assist in rigorously integrating the security perspective into the SE lifecycle and into the design of cyber-physical, technical systems: TRADES (Threat and Risk Assessment for Design of Engineered Systems). The article revises [3] and singificantly extends it. In Section 2, we provide background information related to SSE and its security by design, risk-oriented approach as well as to its model-based design. In Section 3, we describe the TRADES methodology. Then, in Section 4, we discuss applications of TRADES, including representative case studies based on real-life applications of TRADES in industry. Finally, in Section 5, we conclude by reflecting on the methodology and outlining potential extensions and future work.

2. Background

2.1. Systems security by-design

The development of complex, cyber-physical engineered systems1 in industry is typically done within the scope of a development project, in what is often referred to as a project-oriented development approach [1,4]. This development is characterized by a hierarchical approach: the system is depicted as a hierarchical organization of constituents. This view is aligned with concepts of the Vee-model, a prominent system development model [5]. For example, the highest hierarchy of a system comprises several sub-systems; each of these sub-systems includes several lower-level components that may be further decomposed into even lower-level components, and so forth. The hierarchical organization is a means to manage complexity [[6], [7], [8]], as it provides engineering and managerial context and communicates responsibilities with respect to the development effort.
A manifestation of the hierarchical approach is that the development scheme favours a top-down approach [5,[9], [10], [11]]. According to this approach, system-level requirements are established, and are later elaborated with a corresponding design as well as with lower-level requirements. Requirements, therefore, drive the development of the system and its constituents. Throughout the development, the SE team – responsible for the development of a component in a specific hierarchy – defines requirements for the lower hierarchy's components, according to which the lower-level components are designed and eventually verified [7]. These requirements are expected to relate to multiple aspects of functionality, performance and quality; including security aspects.
The United States National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) offers guidelines for introducing security considerations in SE activities [2]. These exhaustive guidelines are stated in natural language, and their implementation requires extensive, dedicated attention by skilled personnel. Such implementation is a burden, and many development efforts and organizations can benefit from a lighter approach to establishing systems security.
In general, the cost of performing changes late in the system development lifecycle or after a system is fielded is increasingly high compared to performing these changes earlier or avoiding them by proper design in advance; and changes that originate from a security perspective are no exception. It is, therefore, important to communicate the need for security mechanisms as early as possible in the development effort [1,[12], [13], [14]].
Also, it is noteworthy that systems development projects may last for several years (typically, the project duration increases with the complexity of the system). During such extended periods of development, the organization – and often, its customers – considers it important to have an up-to-date documentation of the system's design. Furthermore, the resulting systems may remain operational for a long period of time (a 10–20 year service period is not uncommon [15]). Throughout such lengthy development and operation life cycles, the security threat landscape is subject to change (due to newly emerging attack technologies and techniques, for example). This presents a further challenge when one wishes to establish and maintain the up-to-date security posture of systems [16].
The design and assessment of the security aspects of systems are typically done using verbose natural language descriptions, tables and free-form diagrams. These describe the design and/or its potential vulnerabilities, often without properly providing a threat model contextualization. For example, a threat model related publication by The MITRE Corporation for the US Department of Homeland Security, depicts a “representative example” of a credit card processing system as a story, with free-form diagrams and text [17]. Similarly, Munir and Koushanfar describe an approach for the security design of automotive Cyber-Physical System (CPS) – limited in scope to systems using a particular communication bus – using free-form diagram and text, without any formal representation of the system design [18]; Eckhart et al. illustrate the design of a plant, representing the system which comprises CPS components using a free-form diagram as a basis for its security risk assessment [19]; and Bolbot et al. use free-form diagrams and tables for capturing the security design of a ship system [16]. Interestingly, the aforementioned publications do not include any representation of threats in their diagrams, attesting to a gap with respect to considering and communicating threat information within the context of design and as part of the engineering design artifacts. This gap is stated explicitly in a recent systematic literature review of model-based security engineering for cyber-physical systems [20].
A typical way to communicate threat information from a managerial point of view is in the form of a risk matrix. This view is also commonly referred to as “risk heat map” in its colored version. Examples of such representations appear in [16,[21], [22], [23], [24], [25]]. These representations typically reflect the impact (or severity) and difficulty (or likelihood) of a threat, as well as communicate its assessment with respect to acceptable risk levels defined by stakeholders (such as high-level management, risk managers, and regulators). Characteristically, the impact and difficulty levels are qualitatively defined in the form of an ordinal scale (partly due to security risks being hard to assess quantitatively, especially as little data are shared about them [16]). This provides a mechanism to manage security with a risk-based approach, which is considered preferable for CPS applications. However, there is no formal computer model that unifies the risk management perspective with the design effort.
Exacerbating the aforementioned issues is the fact that typical artifacts of Systems Security Design and Assessment (SSDA) are in the form of ad-hoc documentation, and that these may soon become outdated with respect to the actual design of the system, especially in long-lasting or ongoing system development efforts [16]. Moreover, the document-based approach does not support rigorous SE nor the establishing of the security posture throughout the system lifecycle. A representative example of this is a report regarding the security of a radar system [26]: the research explicitly identifies a taxonomy for attack vectors, and yet when the threats are summarized – as a table containing free-form text – some of the attack vectors that appear in the table do not appear in the identified taxonomy.
Software engineering has come up with various mechanisms to depict threat models and perform security assessment of software, some of which are mentioned in the next subsection. However, these mechanisms typically have a software-related technical orientation (e.g., software modeling, programming), which is not widely communicable with different stakeholders. Correspondingly, the security design and assessment of systems from a more conceptual perspective lacks a sound, practical methodology. This is evident in the dominance of the aforementioned free-form analysis approaches.
Furthermore, while introducing required functions for the system and its constituents from a security perspective is often stressed as an important, desirable and effective activity [2,12]; there is no concrete, field-proven mechanism for integrating the security assessment information with the requirements engineering perspective. Bolbot et al. [16], for example, specifically identify the lack of support of security risk assessment approaches for generating system security requirements, and depict a method for allocating these requirements – as desirable functions – to the system and its constituents. However, their suggested method remains unstructured (i.e., no formal model structures are defined) and does not capture the rationale for the introduction of security controls as system functions and how these mitigate risks relating to possible threats.

2.2. Model-based SSDA methodology

With our main goal being the promotion of a rigorous, sustainable depiction of a system's security design, addressing this using a computerized model-based methodology seems essential. A model-based methodology is a set of related processes, methods, and tools to support a discipline – such as SSE – in performing its activities in a model-based context [27]. A model-based approach facilitates the ability to rigorously use digital information models as a single source of reference, establishing communication and coordination between multiple stakeholders [28,29], such as security experts, system engineers, managers, and regulators. More specifically, modelling languages are considered a prospective approach to promote the definition and communication of security-related abstractions [30]. Resulting digital information models can also be maintained throughout the system development, reflecting and communicating any changes to both the system and its security assumptions (e.g., applicable threats, embedded security mechanisms and related risk management decisions).
Prominent model-based approaches to designing secure systems extend the Unified Modelling Language (UML) [31], which is used primarily in software engineering. Accordingly, such approaches require the designer to be proficient with UML modelling as a basis for conducting SSDA. This is unrealistic for SSDA scenarios in which security analysts (alternately, systems security engineers) and systems engineers are not software developers. Furthermore, these approaches are typically oriented towards software systems, and do not address the modelling from a cyber-physical system perspective [32]. One such approach is UMLsec, which extends UML with security-related stereotypes, constraints and tags [33]. Another is SecureUML, which is specifically concerned with role-based access-control (a narrow, low-level security perspective), and it does not support security analysis in high levels of abstraction [34]. Model-Based Security Analysis for Embedded System (MBSAES) is an adaptation of the UML-related System Modelling Language (SysML), designated for the analysis of systems yet not for their design [32]. These three approaches (UMLsec, SecureUML, MBSAES) do not address security requirements elicitation [35]. An alternative to UML-based approaches is an adaptation of the Arcadia model-based method, which maps some cybersecurity domain concepts onto Arcadia SE concepts [36]. The Arcadia-based method does not formally define the model elements and their relations; and the method has yet to be reported as validated (e.g., by introducing case studies). Furthermore, it does not address the essential integration with the risk-related information.
Several model-based approaches to SSE focus on the analysis of a given design. VERDICT relies on a predefined system model as an input, and formalizes a textual, domain-specific language extension for the assessment of cybersecurity aspects [14]. Similarly, AMLSec focuses on structuring engineering data in a textual format, to support automated risk assessment [19]. Promoting code-like schemes, these attempts are developed for software-oriented personnel and promote machine-based applications; yet they ignore common practices of designing systems graphically [37,38]. Consequently, they do not address the need for integrating information between security specialists and systems engineers, especially for those who are not code-oriented or in cases where no previous model-based design artifacts are available. The Cyber Security Requirements Methodology (CSRM) promotes eliciting requirements based on tool-based system descriptions such as SysML diagrams, but provides no formal, model-based approach to perform this [25].
Our research follows some of the previously proposed fundamental research lines for model-based SE [39], and applies them to SSDA. Specifically, it focuses on designing a simple and agile SSDA methodology, as well as on the ability to communicate the design-related security information with different stakeholders; by using a modern, model-based approach.

3. The TRADES methodology

TRADES is a model-based engineering methodology for supporting the design and assessment of systems from a security perspective. We emphasize that model-based engineering refers to the reliance on digital information models for performing engineering tasks. The digital information models are the key artifacts, supporting rigorous recording, construction, communication and analysis of the structured data (which is contained within them). The following subsections describe the TRADES design, while providing justification for the design by relating it to the SSDA domain concepts and context of use. The recording – alternately the storing – of the data is first and foremost by means of structuring them based on a metamodel, as described in subSection 3.1. Model-based representations support the design (alternately, the construction) of the models as well as the communication and the analysis of the design; and these are defined in subSection 3.2. TRADES models are correct by construction, as they rely on explicit, formal definition of a metamodel and of representations. subSection 3.3 describes aspects of the TRADES integrative design. A software implementation – based on the TRADES definitions of metamodel and representations – is available as an open-source modeling environment.2

3.1. The TRADES metamodel

Fig. 1 presents the TRADES metamodel. The metamodel representation is compliant with the metamodeling standard Essential Meta-Object Facility [40]. Elements are depicted as nodes (boxes), and the relationships between these elements are depicted as edges between the nodes.
The metamodel reflects our SSDA ontological concepts. The metamodel elements can be associated with different perspectives, and are denoted – solely for the purpose of providing orientation to the reader – using different colors: threat analysis perspective (red): Analysis, Threat, Threat Allocation, Threat Mitigation; system composition perspective (yellow): Component, Affect Relation, Data, Control; and risk management perspective (green): Score System, Impact Score, Difficulty Score, Score Configuration. We discuss these shortly.
The system-composition-related elements support the detailing of the system to the appropriate level. The “Component” element is used to represent the system and its constituents. A component may include any number of components as its own constituents, providing support for the hierarchical organization of the system. This is denoted using a composition link from Component to itself (the diamond-headed edge, designated “[0..*] components”; its designation means that the edge can be referenced by the element attribute “components” and that its multiplicity is zero to infinity, i.e., a “component” element can include any number of other “component” elements as its constituents). Affect relations can be depicted between components (the “affectRelations” composition relation from the source “Component” to “AffectRelation”; and the “targetComponent” reference relation from “AffectRelation” to the target “Component”). The affect relations are directional data flows, and each may convey any number of data elements (the “data” reference relation to “Data” element). Also, each component may include any number of controls (the “controls” composition relation from “Component” to “Control”). This denotes the assignment of security controls as functions of the component, supporting the typical requirements engineering approach in systems development.
The threat-analysis-related elements support the introduction and management of threat information. “Analysis” is the root model element, which owns the principal component to be analyzed (“component” composition relation pointing to a single element depicting the analyzed system scope). The “Analysis” element also owns threats (the “threats” composition relation) and a scoring system (described shortly; using the “scoreSystem” composition relation). The “Threat” element is used to represent the threats. The threat allocation element is used to assign a threat to a component, representing a possible instantiation of the threat with respect to the system (the component consequently owns the threat allocation, using the “threatAllocations” composition relation; with the threat allocation referencing the threat using a bidirectional reference relation). Threat mitigation is used to associate a control (which owns the threat mitigation) with a specific threat allocation (using a reference relation), denoting the security control's applicability in mitigating the allocated threat.
The risk-management-related elements support the creation of an ordinal risk management scoring system, which owns the definitions of impact scores and difficulty scores. Impact scores are used to rate the impact of a threat allocation when analyzing the system (using the “impactScore” reference relation). Difficulty scores are used to assign a difficulty or likelihood estimation for a threat allocation (using the “difficultyScore” reference relation). The "ScoreSystem" element is used to bind the multiple instantiations of the impact score and difficulty score elements as a scoring system; i.e., a scoring system can include any number of impact scores and difficulty scores; with the ordinal rating of each score element determined by a respective attribute of the element (the “difficulty” integer attribute for DifficultyScore elements and the “impact” integer attribute for ImpactScore elements). This supports the desirable ability to create tailored scoring systems for analyzing different systems in different contexts. For example, a scoring system may be designed to include 4 ordinal impact levels (Low - 1, Medium - 2, High – 3, Critical - 4) and 3 ordinal difficulty levels (Novice - 1, Skilled - 2, Expert - 3). Score Configuration elements are technical elements that promote a model-based coloring of the risk matrix, to result in a heat map version of the matrix. They associate a specific color definition to each cell of the risk matrix (i.e., a selection of a specific impact score and a specific difficulty score), supporting the user's ability to determine the risk perception of intersections between the referenced impact and difficulty scores. Further details follow shortly when we discuss the representations.
Nominal definitions are embedded into the metamodel as attribute enumerations (in blue), and may be adapted for specific uses. “ASSESSMENT” is used to record assessments to both threat allocations and threat mitigations. “CONTROL_STATUS” is used to depict the implementation statuses of the controls.

3.2. The TRADES representations

This section explains the various model-based representations of the TRADES methodology. These representations are defined with respect to the underlying TRADES metamodel, i.e., they are generated based on queries on a digital information model which is an instantiation of the aforementioned metamodel. Demonstrations of the representations follow in the next section (Section 4).

3.2.1. The TRADES design diagram

The design diagram is the main representation of TRADES. It is used for creating, updating and communicating the TRADES information model, and specifically the threat analysis with respect to the system composition. Its notation is overviewed in Table 1 and discussed below.

Table 1. The TRADES design diagram notation.

Domain conceptModel elementNotation in TRADES design diagram
System components (logical/physical)Component
Hierarchical composition“Components” composition relation between “component” and itself
Data flowAffect relation
DataData
(data conveyed by data flow)
Security controlControl
Security control's allocation to component“Controls” composition relation between “component” and “control”
ThreatThreat
Association of threat with componentThreat allocation
Security control's threat mitigationThreat mitigation
System components are used as containers. This design illustrates that components are hierarchical structures, reflecting the responsibility scope. Specifically, the responsibility of a system designer is for the elements that fall within the boundary line of the system's element box-like representation. This SE-oriented design can be contrasted with typical UML-styled designs that associate compositions using composition relation edges (this is evident, for example, in the UML-related design of the metamodel representation, which is depicted in Fig. 1 and described in the previous subsection). Controls may be placed within the relevant component boundary, and are uniquely shaped using a concept-related symbol (shield). System design can be imported from other models using a well-defined model transformation (e.g., [32]), supporting information integration between disciplines and with existing design artifacts. For example, Block-typed elements from SysML models may be translated along with their compositional relations into TRADES component elements with respective “components” relations, and vice versa.
Threats appear as uniquely shaped elements, using another concept-related symbol (cyber-threat). These elements are placed outside the system boundary, corresponding with their conceptual external nature. Threats are allocated to the various components using edges that represent the threat allocation model elements. While typical modeling notations use edges strictly to represent instances of the metamodel's edges, in TRADES we also use edges to represent instances of metamodel nodes. This design promotes the conciseness of the representation, and consequently the ability of non-software personnel to design and communicate the models. This design is well aligned with best practices for design of notation systems [41], specifically the one-to-one mapping between concept and notation. Table 1 further emphasizes this aspect. The use of edges to represent threat allocation elements is a prominent example of the concise design of the notation: in a hypothetical UML-styled implementation, in order to create a new threat allocation the designer would have had to model an instantiation of the threat as a representational node, connect it to the threat using a link (typed according to the language formalism) and then connect the threat instance – using another link – to a component to denote the allocation. This would have required three modeling steps and would have appeared in the diagram as three notational elements. Instead, by using an edge to denote threat allocation, TRADES requires only a single modeling step for doing so, and this also appears as a single notational element. Regardless, the information model is updated automatically to reflect the correct threat allocation construction (i.e., creating a threat allocation element and linking it with both the selected threat and the selected component). Similarly, a threat mitigation edge represents a threat mitigation element, correlating a security control to the threat allocation edge which the control mitigates. We have yet to identify a similar use of an edge pointing to another edge in model-based engineering. Here, this unique design of the representation – with mitigations attached to respective threat allocations – communicates the concept of the threat allocation as a managed risk.
Furthermore, we use a model-based color notation. A color notation enhances the communicability of models, and it is specifically established as effective with respect to communicating aspects of risk assessment [42]. Specifically, threat allocations that are assessed as “Accepted” are colored green, denoting the threat allocation as an acceptable risk; threat mitigations that are assessed as “Accepted” are colored green, denoting they are effective against the threat allocation; and controls are colored according to their developmental status: black for “Proposed”, green for “Implemented” and red for “Rejected.” These definitions may be adapted for specific cases.

3.2.2. The controls implementation map

The controls implementation map is a simple representation that assists in communicating the assignment of security controls to the system constituents, and consequently, the responsibility associated with such an assignment. An example is provided later, in Fig. 6.
The representation is dynamically built based on the specific TRADES model. First, the representation engine queries the model for all component elements and all security control elements and uses the former for creating the rows and the latter for creating columns. We note that the components hierarchy is taken into consideration by indenting the names of the elements in the rows. Then, the cell of each security control intersection with its containing component is marked. Model-based coloring of a cell can be used to denote the different developmental status of the control (alongside the control's status attribute value).

3.2.3. The risk matrix

The risk matrix is the primary representation for communicating the SSDA risk assessment information with high-level management and risk managers. An example is provided later, in Fig. 3.
The representation is derived dynamically from the model's scoring system: a single row is depicted for each impact score element which exists in the information model; a single column is depicted for each difficulty score element which exists in the information model; and each cell is being colored according to the respective score configuration element definition. This design implements the colored version of the risk matrix, once again considering color notation as an effective cognitive mechanism, as established in [42]. It is noteworthy that this risk coloring is model-based; as opposed, for example, to the metamodel diagram coloring of Fig. 1, which is a tool feature that is not model-based. After the table is built according to the scoring system, the cells are populated with all of the model's rated threat allocation elements, based on their associated impact and difficulty scores.

3.3. Integration with public domain information

TRADES is an overall methodology of recording design artifacts and their security rationale and context, and as such it does not specifically include a knowledge base of threats and controls. Instead, it supports and promotes a disciplined use of threat and/or control information derived from expert repositories, such as the public-domain repositories of NIST and MITRE or proprietary, enterprise-specific repositories (as reported shortly in Section 4.2). The integration with formalized expert information about threats and controls is similar to the previously mentioned integration with system design artifacts, and is based on the translation of the relevant information models into TRADES model elements; specifically, into “Threat” and “Control” elements. Furthermore, the TRADES model elements may be enriched with additional attributes to accommodate structured information and metadata imported from the expert repositories. One such implementation is embedded into the aforementioned software implementation of TRADES. It includes a dedicated attribute added to the threat and control elements (via the metamodel) to designate the external source of threat and control elements, accommodating the integration with descriptions of threats and controls derived from two MITRE threat repositories (ATT&CK and CAPEC; the latter is available as a formal information model using XML) as well as with controls descriptions derived from NIST SP800–53 (those are available as a formal information model using the Open Security Controls Assessment Language).

4. Validation

As a validation of TRADES, we discuss several case studies (i.e., using a multiple case study validation methodology). These case studies are representative of the actual use of TRADES as it was performed in a large industrial enterprise, which develops cyber-physical systems, primarily for the defense sector. The first case is a demonstrative example of applying TRADES to a system design case, which is based on real-life cases. Then, we describe the use of TRADES for technical intellectual property (IP) governance, providing two real-life cases (with obfuscation of confidential details).

4.1. Case study I: designing a new cyber-physical system of systems

We discuss a case study of a Ship Missile Defense System (SMDS) design. This case study, which is based on several real-life applications, overviews a typical application of the TRADES methodology and provides concretization of the design and risk matrix representations.
Considering the SMDS as a black-box (e.g., in an early stage of the system development, when only high-level description of the system exists, such as in the system requirements definition phase), we – as security analysts – identify three security-related threats based on our understanding of the system's mission and allocate them to the system: Prevent target detection, Manipulate target detection and Disrupt target interception (Fig. 2-a), with the term “target” referring to a possible physical threat on the system (e.g., a missile or an unmanned aerial vehicle).
Then, we detail some of the system's composition. This can be achieved based on information obtained from communication with SE personnel or from system development artifacts. Fig. 2-b is a typical representation of the system during the system design developmental stage, where system architecture is described. The specific system includes three major subsystems: Radio detection and ranging (RADAR), Weapon control system (WCS) and Interceptor. Some critical information flows between the subsystems are: Plot & Track Report data flow from RADAR to WCS, and Target data flow from WCS to Interceptor. Two of the original threats are reallocated – at this developmental stage – to specific subsystems. The threat allocations are also rated in the model with respect to impact and difficulty. This specific model has three difficulty levels (1 – Low difficulty, 2 – Medium difficulty, 3 – High difficulty) and three impact levels (1 – Low impact, 2 – Medium impact, 3 – High impact), based on desirable risk representation information as agreed upon between the security analyst and a risk management executive. Once the threat allocations are rated, the risk matrix view can be used as a risk management tool to identify the risk level with respect to acceptable levels. This is shown in Fig. 3: the “Prevent target detection on RADAR” is placed in the red area of the risk matrix, representing the crucial need to mitigate it; and the two other threat allocations are mapped within the yellow area, reflecting borderline risk levels that may be in need of further mitigation.
Next, Fig. 2-c illustrates a drill down to a higher resolution of the RADAR subsystem, using a dedicated view. This demonstrates the means to delegate the authority of the design or assessment of this subsystem to someone else, while still keeping the underlying information model aligned. Such a view is representative of the subsystem specification effort of the system development life cycle. Threats that were assigned to the subsystem by the higher-level development hierarchy may be revealed in this view; and specifically in our case the “Prevent target detection” – already assigned to the RADAR – appears. Then, the person responsible for the design of the RADAR may introduce its components, and the person responsible for the RADAR security assessment can assign additional, relevant threats. Based on the threats knowledge base and expertise (which can be RADAR-specific), one such threat is added (“Change detected object properties”) and allocated to the RADAR subcomponent “Detection processing.” A security control function (“Software integrity verification”), which is integrated into the design of the subcomponent, is documented as part of the design description; and its role as a mitigation for the identified threat is explicitly captured in the model.
Fig. 2-d shows the corresponding, synchronized analysis view of the entire model at a later developmental stage. Such a view is often an integrated reflection on the subsystems design and the system design, to make sure these are consistent with respect to system requirements and design goals; and is common in the critical design development phase. It reflects a high-level view of the system and its subsystems, with lower-level threat allocation (specifically, “Change detected object properties” allocated to the lower-level component “Detection processing”) being filtered from the representation to promote a concise representation for the respective level of abstraction. Our analysis of the existing design identifies that vulnerability management should be incorporated into the RADAR development; and it is added as a proposed security control, to document this insight. The “Software integrity verification” mechanism is deemed as an appropriate control to address the “Prevent target detection” threat on the RADAR (in addition to its mitigating role for the filtered lower-level threat allocation). Consequently, the two RADAR security controls are also linked with threat mitigation relations to the threat allocation that they are designed to mitigate, colored green to denote the mitigation is covered by the current system design. With respect to the “Manipulate target detection on WCS” threat allocation, we found no existing security control that can mitigate it, and it is tagged as a “gap,” and accordingly denoted by a red color in the representation. Based on security domain knowledge, we depict that an anomaly detection mechanism may be incorporated into the WCS design to mitigate this threat. This is documented as a proposed security control element within the WCS subsystem, linked as a threat mitigation to the aforementioned threat allocation. The two newly identified security controls are, in fact, desirable system functions and can now be further elaborated in the form of requirements. Furthermore, the mitigation relations may provide some context for elaborating the requirements for the security controls, allowing designers to keep in mind the threat allocations that their designed mechanisms should mitigate.

4.2. Case study II: technical IP governance

This case study demonstrates the value of using a model-based approach for a disciplined SSDA and the reuse of knowledge and definitions in its applications. It also suggests how the data model entity can be used for security assessment and, consequently, for securing the system design in light of this assessment.
The case study is a new, further elaboration of a previously reported technical Intellectual Property (IP) governance case study [43]. IP is explicitly identified as a stakeholder security interest [1]. As depicted in the previous publication, an organization was interested in establishing an IP governance process, and part of this process was to provide design guidelines to system development efforts. The subprocess of providing such guidelines is performed with respect to a preliminary system design; and it relies on an information repository of reverse engineering (RE) techniques as well on IP protection requirements.
In the new case study presented here, we describe the use of TRADES to perform the derivation of the design guidelines. With regard to our implementation, the information repository of RE techniques is a set of threats, established and maintained in the form of a TRADES model; and the IP protection requirements are a system-dependent list of data items and components that embed confidential IP. Each derivation of system design guidelines is performed using a dedicated TRADES model.
Specifically, we use the TRADES design representation. First, we capture the preliminary system design using component, data and affect relation elements. As in the previous subsection, this is done based on communication with the system engineer and/or by reviewing design artifacts. Next, threats are imported from the RE threats information repository, and are allocated to the various component elements, based on cybersecurity engineering expertise. Security controls are introduced based on communication of the threat allocations with the SE team. These security controls are functions designated for implementation in the various development hierarchies; and this can be overviewed by the control implementation map representation throughout the system development. The allocation of security controls to the system and its constituents is – in fact – the design guidelines specification, which further feeds the system development effort as well as serves as the reference for the rest of the IP governance process. Here, risk assessment is performed strictly by using nominal definitions of the threat allocation status (undecided/gap/accepted), with the expected end result being that all threats allocations are “accepted” in terms of their risk.
Fig. 4 depicts the application of TRADES to design the IP protection for an undisclosed system, which we refer to as “System X.” The system includes two subsystems; with one of the subsystems being further decomposed into three components. One relevant data flow is captured, conveying the data item “Data1.” Five different threats – originating from the RE threats information repository – are allocated to the system and its constituents, resulting in five threat allocations; whereas one threat remains unallocated (i.e., deemed irrelevant for this specific case). Some of these threats are adapted versions of threat information derived from MITRE's public repository CAPEC,3 which contains structured information about attack patterns: RE Type4 is an adapted version of CAPEC-37: Retrieve Embedded Sensitive Data, RE Type5 is an adapted version of CAPEC-75: Manipulating Writeable Configuration Files, and RE Type6 is derived from CAPEC-189: Black Box Reverse Engineering. The mitigation of the threat allocations is designed as incorporation of five security controls. Each control is assessed as an effective mitigation against a specific threat allocation, and therefore all mitigation relations appear green. Consequently, all threat allocations are assessed as “accepted” (due to the attached mitigation relations), and therefore appear green in the model-based representation.
Fig. 5 depicts the application of TRADES to design the IP protection for another undisclosed system, which we refer to as “System Y.” This system comprises two subsystems, with two data flows identified as relevant for IP governance. The two subsystems are adopted from System X, with SubsystemA now including two additional components: ComponentA4 and ComponentA5. The latter component is the origin of the additional data flow, conveying “Data2” and “Data3”; and it includes an implemented security control “Control6.” These are all documented in the model, using the design diagram. As several controls were already introduced in the design of the different components (in the aforementioned project of developing System X, which preceded the System Y development project), their statuses in the model appear as “implemented,” and, therefore, they appear as green symbols in the model-based representation (as opposed to black control symbols, denoting a “proposed” control). The same threats – imported from the RE threats information repository – appear, with all previously identified threat allocations assessed as relevant due to the reuse of components. In addition, the “RE Type5 information” threat is now allocated to one of the new components (ComponentA4), and the “RE Type3 Software” threat is allocated to the other, newly introduced component (ComponentA5). This results in seven threat allocations.
The mitigation design is also more comprehensive here compared with the previous project. It includes a wider set of security controls allocated to the different components. Specifically, the guidelines for the implementation of ComponentA3 – which is reused from System X – now include additional security controls. In light of the newly analyzed data flow (conveying data items “Data2, Data3”), the security expert considered it essential to introduce further mitigations to the “RE Type3 Software to ComponentA3” threat allocation. One control – Control7 – was rejected by the development team, thus appearing in red. An alternative control was proposed in the form of Control9. Similarly, the proposed security control for ComponentA4 – Control8 – was rejected due to the inability to change the component's design; consequently leading to an alternative suggestion to incorporate Control10 in the higher, subsystem hierarchy. Both Control8 and Control10 are derivatives of the informal mitigation technique offered in the CAPEC threat information (“Implementation: Integrity monitoring for configuration files”).
The developmental status of the assigned controls for System Y are depicted using the Control implementation map in Fig. 6. The figure showcases the representation and its ability to reflect the overall development situation, which is typically coordinated by a systems security engineer.

5. Discussion

In this article we presented the TRADES methodology, addressing the crucial need for a disciplined, yet practical, model-based approach which supports systems security assessment and design. Various representations promote the holistic view offered by the methodology, while relying on a single, well-defined metamodel. A software implementation of the methodology is available, and attests to the methodology's rigor and soundness, and, consequently, its technical validity. Several case studies were reported as validation, based on real-life applications of the methodology.
Table 2 provides a comparative analysis between TRADES and related approaches. TRADES is the only methodology which offers an integrated, consolidated view of the systems engineering perspective and the security perspective. It integrates threat modeling with system design to support security by design, preferably as a top-down, scalable and tailorable approach. TRADES offers a formal approach to implementing the security by design aspects of NIST SP 800–160 [2]. Accordingly, TRADES explicitly and formally identifies required security functions in their context, as opposed to the particular, bottom-up approach of CYber-Risk Assessment for Marine Systems (CYRA-MS), which only informally identifies security controls and avoids their allocation [16]. TRADES relies on a single information metamodel. It is the only model-driven methodology to include multiple, rigorously defined and generated representations, addressing the different viewpoints that are essential to communicate the multi-faceted aspects of SSE. By comparison, CYRA-MS provides representations for system design and risk management but these are informal (not based on a metamodel); CSRM provides a formal system design representation but only an informal risk management representation [25]; and other model-based approaches – such as UMLsec and the approach by Navas et al. [36] – focus mainly on system design representations. Of particular interest is MBSAES [32], which includes a formal system design representation but only an informal attack representation. Consequently, the MBSAES representations are left unsynchronized and unconnected, as opposed to the inclusion of both the design and the threat perspectives in the TRADES design representation. Furthermore, only VERDICT and the approach of Eckhart et al. (of the previous state of the art methodologies) incorporate scoring system details into their modelling languages, albeit in a very limited form: the former employs hardcoded definitions (e.g., prespecified values for “severity”), and the latter supports nominal definitions of consequence but not of feasibility/likelihood. TRADES provides a full mechanism to create a user-defined scoring system that is integrated into the model and supports establishing and maintaining the typical risk matrix viewpoint. Like other model-based modeling methodologies, TRADES does not cover the specific domain knowhow with respect to threat modeling or risk management, and this should be approached by enacting complementary methods, such as the informal STRIDE for security requirements elicitation [44], the informal STAMP-based analysis for security control design [45] and the informal ranking method suggested as part of CYRA-MS. The integration of domain knowhow can also be achieved by integrating information from specializing repositories – such as those by MITRE and NIST – that offer some semi-formal artifacts (e.g., XML files of threat information and security controls). TRADES provides the mechanisms for accommodating such expert information knowhow into an integrative, actionable model (unlike other methodologies, such as MBSAES). An example of such integration appears in our second case study, where several threats are based on CAPEC-based threats. Future research or applicative adaptations of TRADES may seek to elaborate the definitions of relevant TRADES model elements – specifically Threat and Control – to align them with popular meta-level definitions used by specializing repositories, e.g., by adding relevant attributes to the model elements.

Table 2. Comparative analysis. “-“ designates lack of support; “I” – designates support by informal definition; “F” – designates support by formal definition; “(P)” – designated support is partial/limited.

Criteria vs. MechanismNIST SP 800–160 [2]MITRE's CAPECNIST 800–53UMLsec [33]Navas et al. [36]CSRM [25]CYRA-MS [16]MBSAES [32]VERDICT [14]AMLsec [19]TRADES (this work)
Addressed conceptsThreatIF–(P)F(P)IIIIF(P)FF
Threat allocation / riskI–––FIIIF(P)FF
Scoring systemI––––II–(P)F(P)FF
Security ControlIIF–FI(P)I–F–F
ComponentI––FFFIFFFF
Hierarchical compositionI––F(P)I–(P)IF(P)FFF
Security control allocationI––(P)FFI––––F
Data flowI––(P)FF–I–F–F
DataI––(P)FF–I–(P)F–F
Addressed representationSystem design–––(P)F(P)FFI(P)F for system, I for threats––F
Risk Management–––––(P)II–––F
Function allocations / Development status––––––––––F
Specialized knolwedgeThreat–F––––––(P)F(P)F(P)F
Security ControlsIF–––––(P)F(P)F(P)F
Validation–––Case studies–A Single case studySingle case studyCase studiesSingle case studySingle case studyTwo case studies (based on multiple industrial applications)
With early development stages typically being incoherent and unstructured [46], TRADES provides a disciplined approach for introducing and communicating security functions early in the system development process and for tracking progress. In support of the hierarchical, top-down system development approach, TRACDES provides a mechanism to portray the system and its components using the same graphical language, as well as an abstraction mechanism to view the details that are pertinent to the required hierarchical level and support delegation of authority (e.g., Fig. 2-c vs. Fig. 2-d). Regardless of the view, the underlying model remains consistent; as the integration between multiple expert views and information relies on a rigorously defined metamodel which encompasses relevant ontological concepts (as opposed, for example, to the technically informal approach of CSRM [25]). Furthermore, the TRADES metamodel accommodates integration with other information models, such as SysML models and structured descriptions of threats and security controls.
TRADES is designed for supporting the dominant hierarchical, top-down system development methodology, which is well established by prominent systems engineering literature (such as [5]), by security engineering literature (such as [2]) as well as by certification authorities. The overarching system development methodology emphasizes the explicit definition of requirements as a driver of the development and the compliance with the requirements as the indicator of achievement. Accordingly, TRADES assists in defining and managing functional security requirements. Consequently, we estimate that TRADES can be of value to any development methodology that is requirements-oriented and considers the identification and/or introduction of security controls as functions. We provided two types of case studies to demonstrate the validity of using TRADES in different, requirements-oriented development schemes. We suggest that future research efforts assist in establishing external validation of using TRADES in additional system development schemes.
Representative case studies of two types were shown. Case study I described the TRADES-based integration of information from decision makers and risk management (acceptable/unacceptable levels of risks), security engineering (threats and potentially mitigating security controls) and systems engineering (system architecture). Case study II described the TRADES-based integration of information from policy and governance (threats, including derivation from external threat information repository), cybersecurity engineering (threat allocations), systems engineering (architecture) and engineering management (development status of suggested controls). After two years of using TRADES in the defense industry, multiple applications of each of the two types were recorded in CPS development contexts: over 4 applications of the first type, covering a satellite system, a plane and remotely piloted aircraft systems; and over 6 of the second type, covering RADAR systems, a navigation system, remotely piloted aircraft systems, and a missile system.
TRADES provides a realistic, domain-specific model-based approach, which does not impose on other domain experts to use model-based engineering. Once security controls are allocated as functions, requirements engineering and system design can take the traditional form of natural language requirements or be performed using other model-based techniques (e.g., the SysML-derived approach suggested by Wach and Salado [47]).
We have demonstrated the advantages of using a model-based design approach, specifically the employment of meaningful, synchronous representations to address various needs of stakeholders as well as the ability to reuse elements from other models. We intend to further extend the model-based design capabilities by improving our tool ecosystem. The formally defined metamodel of TRADES accommodates integration with additional data sources. This has already been integrated into the implementing software tool, making threat-related public-domain knowledge (from MITRE repositories) accessible directly within the modeling tool. Further research is planned to support the automation of the system security assessment using this knowledge. This can be achieved by adapting the mechanisms suggested by Eckhart et al. [19] to assess TRADES models or by incorporating a formal ontology based approach [[14], [48]]. We are also currently incorporating model-based validation rules into the tool, in order to provide users with suggestions and insights regarding their designs and the system security postures.
Our case studies illustrate the use of TRADES in early-stage system development scenarios, focusing on design aspects. The first case study exemplifies how TRADES can be used throughout early development: from system requirements definitions, through preliminary system design and subsystem specification, and onto critical system design. The second case study exemplifies the TRADES mechanism which allows to keep track of the developmental status in support of security-related decision making. While the control status model concept (“CONTROL_STATUS”) and its manifestation in the various representations provide the infrastructure for using TRADES throughout complete development life cycles – and possibly even during operations; such use has yet to be fully explored. Future research is likely to elaborate TRADES so that it can be used even more comprehensively in full system development and operation life cycles. This may involve further integration with information from Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) system descriptions as well as from disclosed security vulnerabilities information systems (such as MITRE's CVE), both of which are pertinent to the implementation phase and the operation phase of the systems’ life cycle.
